speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Edward Swenson came to the Park Valley with his parents in 1891 at the age of eight. They were one of the first families to settle in this little Norwegian community, a remote clearing surrounded by wilderness until potlatch logging came in the teens. Ed describes their first trip from Moscow to Park, building their homestead cabin in the first survey, the appearance of the valley and its first two settlers, experiences with neighbors and Indians, and the tribulations of building a road which would connect Park with the world outside.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: ...Norway and decided to come to the United States? Do you know why your parents decided to leave Norway and to come here?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes, my father's older brother came before they did and found land in Minnesota, you know. And to the people back in Norway, 160 acres of land would be a big farm. And they heard they could get land on a homestead here, so they decided to go. They saved up enough money for the voyage, you know. He came and his brother met them in New York and they came on through. They lived three years in Wisconsin to get a start for homesteading, you know, and then moved into Minnesota and located there with his brother as a neighbor, so they worked together in building up, you know, and got started there. They lived there about 19 years, I believe it was, before they came to Idaho.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know, ever hear, about what the conditions were like in Norway? Was it pretty hard in Norway?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, so far as I learned from the folks, it was not bad at all at that time. But of course, for a young man to get started there wasn't so easy because the land was all taken up pretty well, you know, and father wanted to be a farmer. That was what he learned there, so that's how they came then. And the ship that they came on was a sail ship, of course, and it was the maiden voyage for that ship. It had never been on the ocean before, and so when they got pretty well out on the ocean, they had run into a storm and for three days they had to tackle back and forth against it to keep from going back, you know, and it was pretty rough. Father got seasick, but not so bad, but we got over it, and mother had the two children, you know, to take care of. She said it didn't bother her so much.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So then they went to Wisconsin for a few years, and then Minnesota for quite a while, but then they decided to leave Minnesota.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did they decide to come to Idaho?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, the storms were so bad there. They lost two crops of grain two years in succession, and that discouraged them pretty much. They had heard about this Idaho country, you know, and they decided they wanted to come west. My oldest brother went first. He went in the fall of 1800, 1890 I mean to say, and then we came in the spring of 91 and settled there together and built up again. So the folks had quite an experience in pioneering.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: They did it more than once. They did it more than once.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, you see, they came out there on the prairie in Minnesota, no town nearby, and they had to build up the best they could right in the wilderness. There had been an Indian War the year before they came there, but that was settled so they were safe in that respect.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Then no neighbors around close by, you know. They started with oxen and wagon to get to town, and that took a long time, you know. Mother had to be alone there with the little children until they get back, so it wasn't so good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did they decide to come out here? What means did they use to get out?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: We came on an immigrant train from, I think it was, Wilmer, Minnesota to Troy, Idaho. But in the meantime, there had been a slide of land onto the railroad track between Troy and Moscow, so we had to get up at Moscow and go by wagon from there on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what that trip was like from by wagon out to the park country?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: My oldest brother left Memphis there, you know, with team and wagon, and we'd go parkways. It took us three days from Moscow to our Idaho. Very bad roads, you know.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: My brother and I walked much of the time, and my father walked most of the time. So what little baggage we had was on the wagon, and my mother and my sister would ride in the wagon. We came to,

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: there was a farmstead about, I'd say about a mile east from Jewel down there. Actually, it was a station there, a railroad station. It stayed the first night. The second night, we got out to Texas Ridge and stayed there, and it took us about a day from there to get over to park. When we got down to the river, the horses had to ford the river. There was no bridge, you know, and the water was high. And they had a foot log there for us to walk across. And when they got across the river, they had to take the wagon apart and use just the hind part of the wagon to carry a few things they could load on there to get up the hill on the east side. And then the next day, they came back to get the rest of the things. There wasn't much road there. So we walked all the way across the canyon until we got over the park. It was just about sunset when we came to my brother's place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you cross the canyon? Pardon? Where did you cross the canyon?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: There was a farmstead named Buckaloo on the west banks of the canyon, and there was a road down from their place. So some of our things were left there, and there was kind of a makeshift road. It wasn't graded much, so they had to rough lock the hind wheels on the wagon to hold it back, you know. And they had chains along. They cut down a little fir tree and tied it behind the wagon to help hold it back and made it down the hill. I can remember that because I thought it was so odd to see that tree dragging behind the wagon, you know. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About where would that be today?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: From Derry, it would be just about four miles south and then east. We didn't go by Derry at those times, you know. There was a road further south. We got across on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did they go so far? Why did your father decide to go all the way to Park?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Because my oldest brother was there, and two of his wife's brothers were there at Park. And they had formed a land connected together, you know. And of course we wanted to be with them, so they went across there and it was so nice. The valley laid there some nice timber scattered over and not much underbrush, you know. And it looked so nice that they wanted to be there. And that was why later on when they formed a post office there, they named it Park.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you expand on what that country looked like when you first saw it? What it looked like to you?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, from the first beginning, there was nothing. There wasn't even any fences or anything, you know. The land wasn't surveyed yet. They each settled where he thought there might be a dividing line. Then eventually they got a survey ran across from a section line on Texas Ridge straight through the valley. And that was staked out. And then the farmers would measure out from that section line, north and south. And get pretty well located until it was surveyed permanently.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I can remember Father was with the surveyor and when they came to a section corner, they were running a line east and west. When they came to a section corner, that was not far from where Father had built a house. So Father asked the surveyor to view out the line north and south through what would be his west line. And there had been timber burnt down and there was a big pine stump by itself. And that happened to be right on the line, on the west line of our place. So the surveyor told him, that'll be your landmark for your west line. Then he measured up from this section corner to get the south line. And then he started from there. So, but some of the farmers there would be kind of off. They would be too close to the neighbor when they finally got their lines run. So they would move back then into a better location. My father had, they built a long house and he had the logs built up pretty well the height they wanted. And when they measured up, we were about five or ten yards in on our neighbor's place. So they had to tear the logs down and move them.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: But that didn't take long because they marked each log for each side, you know. And as they moved them up to the permanent side, the logs would be laid on the side where they had been before and just roll them back up again.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: They didn't have the roof on the place when, by the time he realized it, he had to move it.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: The roof wasn't on yet when they had to move it. So it didn't take long to move the walls, you know.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And the foundation was just a log. They smoothened the ground out and started with a log on the bottom and up. And then they knocked in the floor joists about a log high off of the ground. So the floor would be up from the ground a ways. I can remember when they built that, I wasn't able to do any work, but I was always with Father, you know, when he was working there. Mother was staying with my oldest brother, Mother and my sister. But wherever the men went, I was right with them there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it take them very long to build?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Several neighbors get together, you know, and it wouldn't take long. They knew how to fit the logs together and it didn't take long. They cut down green trees and peeled the logs and notched them together. And they found cedar. Their cedar was the best to split for shakes, you know, for the roof. And so they split shakes and put on for the roof. It didn't cost very much to build those days, except a lot of hard work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell me that your father found a man that really knew how to use the broad axe to do this?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes. Yes, that was a neighbor. He had come in there just after we did. So we lived in the old cabin, you know, for I think about eight years. And so Father made a deal with this man to hew the logs for what we call the new house, you know. And there was no money exchanged. This man needed a lot of fence rails to fence his landing. And we had cedar there to make rails from. So Father made a deal with him to pay with fence rails. The man wanted $20 to hew the logs for the house. And fence rails was valued at a cent each then, 12 feet long. So Father made 2 ,000 rails in payment for hewing the logs.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And by that time we had horses and wagon, and so we delivered the fence rails over to the man's place. And he stayed with us. He had room and board with us while he hewed the logs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well getting back to that first, the old cabin that you built, getting back to the old cabin, the first one, was that the first thing that your family did when they got there, was build?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: He located a spring of water not very far from the house and then he got ready to build. And you see we came in the spring of the year and before fall we were in our own home there. And then he built a barn and chicken house and things like that. He had to stay home and work all that summer. Then the next year when harvest time came, he went out and worked in the harvest fields through the harvest. And then back and away they would work on the farm through winter, falling trees and clearing land, you know, to get broke up next summer. So there wasn't much idle time in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did he go to work in the harvest?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Out in what's called the Genesee Valley. It's farmland lived where they were about seven miles north and east from Genesee where he worked. And they raised mostly wheat there and they used binders to cut it with and then they stacked it. Then when all the district was stacked, then they'd start threshing. So he worked until they got through stacking. He was good at stacking grain and he worked through that.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So he'd just go out for the harvest season and then he came back right away.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes, he'd come back then so they could, they'd put up hay. There was the wild grass grew tall there. There was no cattle much around, you know, just a cow or two for milk. There was not any young cattle running there then, so the grass grew up good. And he was pretty good with a hand scythe and he'd go out and cut the grass and we boys wrecked it up into little bunches, you know, shocked it up and then hauled it home. There was so much open land so you'd go almost anywhere and cut the hay, you know. There wasn't much brush or other small trees. It was really nice there. You cut grass among those big pine trees, you know, after all.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: We had only one cow the first winter. Then the next year we had more. But it took a few years before he got enough ground broke up, you know, to raise any hay. So we had that grass. And I remember one winter we were short of hay for the cows and the cows liked the moss that grew on the pine trees. And they were cutting down trees to clear the land anyway so the cows would come and eat the moss off of the branches. And then he fed him hay at night. So we made through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This land being so clear of brush, is that because the Indians kept it burnt?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, there were stories about that, that the Indians would set fires when they were coming back out of the woods. But there were also fires from lightning, thunderstorms, which we knew happened after we came there. We could see trees on fire once in a while after a rainstorm. And of course that would set the grass on fire. We kind of believed that that was pretty much what did that. It was claimed that the Indians didn't want the brush to grow because it was easier for them to ride through. But they formed trails, you know, when they went back into the mountains. They had one trail up from Kendrick and up through and that trail came through our land. Almost through the center of it, quite close to where we had built our house. They came through there and going back into the woods to hunt and fish and pick berries. And they had a camping place right close to where Father built the barn. And one day they camped there, they staked their horses. We had broke up a few acres then and had seeded two oats for hay. But they stayed on the same camping place and staked their horses right in the oat field. The oats were out maybe five or six inches high and they settled down to eat there. Father and the older boys were away that day, so it was Mother and my sister and I. And some mother told me, she said, you better go and tell them to move their horses off of the oats. Well, I had heard the Indians were wild, you know, and I was pretty well scared of it. But I managed to get over there and showed them where they should stake their horses, you know. And they did. They started to move, put their horses over. And I ran back to the house and Mother told me, she said they were nice to you, so she said, ask them to come in and have their meal. Well, of course, I couldn't talk to them, but somehow or other I got the message across. So they came to the house. There was five or six of them. And Mother set the table out so they could eat, you know, at the table. But they put the table back where it was and they sat down on the floor in a circle and had their dinner. And Mother had coffee for them and sugar and cream and things like that, you know, a little fruit. So they really enjoyed it and they managed to try to talk, you know, to each other. So then they went away and in the fall when they came back they stopped to buy deer hides. You know, there was no season on deer, so when you were sure to meet you go out and shoot a deer. And my older brother, he had a gun that he could shoot deer with, so we always had deer meat when we wanted it. And we'd heard about this that the Indians would buy the hides, so we had deer hides on hand. They would pay fifty cents for a deer hide. But Mother thought that wasn't enough money, so she held out for seventy -five cents. And they threw motions and that's why they began to talk trade, you know, and she finally got the seventy -five cents.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they pay in money or did they pay in trade?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh yeah, they had money with them.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I don't know. You know, they used to make up moggins and things like that and sell to the white people, so they'd have a little money. And of course then afterwards we fenced the landings so they had to follow the roads back in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many families would you say were there when you got there?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I think there were about six families in there that next summer after we came. I couldn't say just what there was when we came because I don't remember that, but as they came in they began to get acquainted, you know, and they started back and forth. And I can remember there was about six right in the main part of the valley there. And they began to help each other, you know. As each new settler came, the others would go and help them get started and neighbor with them. We didn't have any schoolhouse the first year we were there. The second year, that would be in 1892, they built a schoolhouse by going together and getting logs and built up a log schoolhouse. So that fall we had a little school, but it wasn't until next spring that we really had a school term. But I can remember going to school after snow came in the fall. But then they waited until next spring then. So I was between nine and ten years old before I went to school. And I couldn't speak English. So the school was in English, of course. We lived in a Norwegian settlement back east, you know, back in Minnesota. But it didn't take long. We had a very good teacher. I can remember the first teacher we had, teacher named Ramsey. And he was very good with us children. He seemed like he was like one of us, you know. So we got along pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the other families Norwegian too when you first got there?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I know four of them were. There was one family. They were supposed to be French Canadians. Their language was a little different. I mean their English language was mixed a little with French words, you know. But they spoke pretty plain English anyway. And so they, but they didn't have children old enough to go to school until after they'd lived there a while. Their first child was born after they settled there. So they were among the smaller ones when I quit school there.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I went to school until I was about 14 years old. By that time the older boys were out on their own pretty much, you know, and I had to help father with the work. So I had to quit school. We had our school terms in the spring then, you know, in the summer. We had only four months of school at first. So it didn't take long. But we managed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this teacher hired from the outside?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes. And they, they boarded with some of the settlers in there then, you know. I can remember towards the end of the years that I went to school, we had a, I can't remember, I think her name was Longfellow. And she boarded with the Norwegian family. She couldn't speak Norwegian, but they managed that. And she had to pay $45 a month for boarding room. And she was getting $60 a month for teaching.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She was getting $60 a month for teaching?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So she didn't have much left by the end of the year?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No, but you know, a dollar was pretty big at those times. And wages were small.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And she walked three quarters of a mile to come to school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about this fellow that you mentioned to me before that you called a land grabber?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: This guy, he sounds like a real character in the early days.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, they had located there before we came. It was on his land that Father first started his cabin. And when the survey was made, that section line came right by his house. Just a few routes from the house. So he was lucky to be built so he didn't have to move. And so we had to move again. And the reason why he was kind of a loner was when he moved in there, he had a little money. And he brought a carload of cattle and furniture and stuff, you know, horses and wagons. And so he was considered a little richer than the rest of them. And I guess it kind of went to his head because he sort of acted like he was better than the rest of them. And that didn't go very good because the others were good neighbors, exchanged work and all, but he didn't. So it kind of created a friction there, you know. And he, you know, in those times, law enforcement wasn't as strict as it was later on. A man could carry a gun anytime he wanted to, and if he got crowded, he might be tempted to use it. So he began to get a little bit afraid of some of his neighbors there, so he strapped a gun on his hip and carried that around with him. And of course that didn't help any to be a neighbor, so. But eventually, he began to see that he didn't gain anything by it, so he put his gun away and became a neighbor.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: You told me that he got into a big argument about where the school should ought to be built.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes, they started building a school, and he thought it was too far from his place. And he began to object, and so there was another Norwegian joining him on the west of his land, and so he offered to give an acre of land to put the school over there. And the majority ruled on that. Well, he fought that, and he finally pulled his gun, but he didn't have time to use it. One of the men took the gun away from him and emptied the shots in the gun and stuck it in his own pocket. So they went to work and moved the logs over to the other place and started building there. When they went home in the evening, he gave the gun back to this fellow. After that, he didn't carry it when he came to work.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, of course, there was a little fistfight included with him before they settled. He didn't stand in the show there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did they call him the land grabber because he tried to grab the good land?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes, before the survey was made, he had run a temporary fence around over 200 acres of land there, you know.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And so when the survey was made, he had to move his fences.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: He had a boy, one of his, the oldest boy in that family, was the same age as I was. Well, at first, I kind of shied away from him, but we became good friends. We were in the same class in school, so we had no trouble at all. And eventually, they began to get more neighborly. This man's wife and my mother were good friends. They got to miss each other and were really good friends. So the rest of us, like my sister and their girls, were good friends, you know. We began to neighbor back and forth, and things were pretty good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where, what part of Park did he stake out for his place?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I take it he was close to the first person in there and had a chance to take the place that he wanted for his own.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, each one when he came in, he just walked around there until he found where he'd like to be, and that's where they located.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did he pick? Where did this guy pick for his land?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: He was right in the center of the valley.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: He was one of the first ones, and he located right in the center there pretty well. Well, there were others just about as close in, but I mean, the first group, you know, that came in there would locate in the center, and he was one of them. There were two others, I know, that were right close in. The line came pretty close to the buildings of the first three settlers there, three or four, and he was one of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the, how would you describe the shape of the Park Country, what it looks like and how big it is?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, the biggest part of the valley laid quite level. There were a couple little creeks of water running through during high water time, and pretty well into summer, but in later years they dried up. So that was why, too, that they settled like that. They all wanted to be close to water, you know, and these little streams run through the land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And it was, the timber was mostly yellow pine growing in the open and with grass?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes, on the flat there was mostly yellow pine, and there was fir thickets, young trees. The fir likes to grow where there's moisture, so wherever the land would hold moisture along, that's where the fir trees would get started growing. So there was thickets of fir tree and then yellow pine scattered.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: They were not so, there wasn't so much trees there that what you would look down through the valley, you know, all over there. That's why it looked just like a park, like the trees had been planted there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then around the park area itself was forest, was woods?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Oh yes. See, the valley was surrounded on three sides by mountains, and the timber would grow down onto the flats. Whenever the snow melted in the spring, a lot of that would seep down onto lower land, you know, so there was moisture for the timber to grow. But out on the main valley, the main level of land, wouldn't be so much moisture, so the trees were more scattered.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you depend on the wild game a lot in those days?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Of course we did, because nobody had cattle to speak of. As they started settling there, they would bring a milk cow or two, and it took a while to have beef cattle. And so it was venison, and they had grouse and patridge, and then there were some meadows east from there about five miles, and in high water in the spring there would be salmon come up there. So the men would go back there with their spears and bring home a few salmon. So we had a variety.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And of course when they go out to town, if they had to have other meat, they'd bring home some from there, but very seldom they would have to buy meat at the market.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: There was no season on game. You could kill as many as you wanted. And the first summer that my older brother lived out there, he traded venison for meat at the meat market in Troy. He'd go out and shoot some deer and dress them up and take them to town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that that was the main part of your diet, would be the venison and other things that you shot in the wild in the first years? Or would you have a lot of store -bought food too?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No, venison was the main meat supply. In the spring they'd have salmon, and then of course they had patridge and grouse for a change.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: So there were bears scattered here and there before there were any dogs in the valley where bears would be roaming around. But it seemed like people at that time didn't take to bear meat very much because they didn't need it. But occasionally there were some that would kill a bear and use that meat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now for supplies in the early years when you were first there, where did you go and how did you get there?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, we had Kendrick on the southwest from us and Troy on the west side. There wasn't much road to either place, but the neighbors got together and talked over which would be the best route to take to town. Because they had to build the roads, you know. So Troy was chosen as the best town. It was the biggest town then. Troy was built before Kendrick was. So the farmers went together and they chose a couple men to represent the district to go to Troy to see what deal they could make with the merchants there. Because they needed to have a supply of provisions, groceries to have while they were working on the road. They couldn't go out and earn money any other way then, you know. It took a lot of time and work to build that road, especially across the Powletch Canyon. So the grocers in Troy agreed to furnish groceries and dynamite for blasting. So the men could spend their time working on that. By that time the settlement had grown so there were probably a dozen or more of good young men to work on the road. They had horses and plows for plowing up wherever they could. But in the canyon they couldn't use much of that. They used the horses to kind of drag the trees away from where they were building the road. They'd cut down trees and there were trees laying down and they'd cut off enough of the tree to have a space to drive through, you know. And they'd use the horses to drag those logs out of the way. The rest of it was hand work. There was a bachelor there that had done some mining and he was familiar with using dynamite so when they were running to Rockway he would do the blasting. So they went on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did the road go to connect up with the main road there?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: It was a little further up the river, further north. There were some homesteaders on the west side named Morris, two brothers and their mother and father. They had a road out to their place, you know, out on the breaks of the canyon. So the people that parked then continued on from that road down across the canyon.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: There was no bridge there neither the first few years but the main thing was to get the road. They could ford the river. So after they had the road built then they built a bridge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this road very switchbacky?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes, they had on the west side was quite a flat, there was no what you might call creeks running down to cause any depression in the soil there. So they could go quite a ways. They made only three switchbacks on the west side to get up. But on the east side there were fifteen switchbacks. They followed the ridge pretty well on the east side to go up. And those switchbacks were, they were switchbacks okay. They were turned pretty short. When you got to dig into a steep side hill to make a turn you don't dig very wide. But they made it. I know you couldn't drive four horses around those switchbacks. So they managed to get along anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was hand tools and muscle power, huh?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Pick and shovel and axe and cross cut saw. That was the equipment they had.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I imagine that it took a real long time to put it in too.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I can't remember how long it took because I was only about nine, ten years old then, you know. So I don't remember that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About where did it connect up with the road in Detroit?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, you know, the land settled up as a rule. They start close to town to settle up, you know. And as they settled back where they built roads through. And so that was, the road was built clear out to the breaks of the Potlice Canyon. So then when we got connected up we followed their roads on to Troy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Before that time, where did the people trade before they put that road in?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Some went to Kendrick and some went to Troy. They had to go horseback and pack the goods in and, you know. And there was a post office located pretty well towards the first road they had in there. And they had a little grocery store there. So the men would go horseback across there and load the groceries on their saddle and pack it home that way. And get the mail there until they got the other road built.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this Anderson?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Anderson was the name. That was the name of the people that homesteaded there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there an actual trail there or would it just be a cross country?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, I suppose they cut a kind of a trail through, you know, so they could go through with a pack horse.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: The first two men that ever came to that valley were two miners. Big Husky men. One was named Jurgensen and the other one was named Goldstrom. Jurgensen was a Norwegian and Goldstrom was a Swede. And they walked across the canyon. They were prospecting. They walked across the canyon and came in on that valley and it looked so nice in there they decided to settle there. So they staked out where they wanted to be and then they went back to bring in supplies. They even carried their own cookstove in on their back and other supplies that they needed. Built a cabin and settled down. They were pretty close to the edge of the canyon there where they settled.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they still there when your family came?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, they were there when we came. Of course the stoves that they carried across were not the full -size cookstove. They were little bit of things that they could carry but they would do when there was just one man in each house, you know. They managed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would a guy have to lead his horse and pack the supplies on top of it?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Well, not when we came. They had that first road where we came through with a wagon, you know. So they would use that road until they built the other one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that road went fairly close to Anderson?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: It was further north than Anderson. If I remember correctly, I'm not sure about this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about this other road?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: You told me that there was another one that wound up somehow getting built in a crazy location.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yes, that was about 1908 or 9. There was a little friction there about that. Some of the neighbors there wanted a certain route close to their place, you know. And the others wanted another route. So we got a surveyor to come in and he was more friendly to the people on the, what we call the east road, east route. That went north across the mountain and over towards Helmer after the potlatch had built their road through. So they made a survey on that route. When the surveyor got to the top of the mountain, he began to look around and he saw there was no good place to go down without a lot of grading. So he stopped there and he went back to Moscow and reported in on that. Then it wasn't long until there was another route started and he went back and surveyed a part of that and it would have been a much better route. By that time, election came and he didn't get elected for the next term. They got another man that voted in and he went in there and he voted in favor of the east route and that was built. But it just wasn't used very long because by that time the potlatch company built a road into park for logging, built a railroad in for logging out their timber. As soon as that was finished, when they pulled the rails out of there, the settlers in there built that into a permanent road to market.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I believe that road is still being used.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: What you say sounds like it was just a very isolated piece of country in the early days.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Between us and the market, yes. On that route, that was mostly just timber.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: I remember we had to get permission from the potlatch lumber company to build that road because they owned the timber through there. They objected to having timber cut down just to build a road. So we made up a petition and took it over to potlatch to get their signature. I was appointed to take that over there. William Dery, the head of the, had his office there, but that was the main office. There was another office that you came into first. I forget the name of that office or the manager of that office, but anyway, I presented my petition there and he looked at it and he said that he would take it into the main office. He took it in there and it was turned down immediately. Mr. Dery said he would have no road built across that on account of danger of fires being set by people traveling through. Well, I got a bright idea, so I told him I should think that road would be an advantage through there because if any lightning fire or other fires should get started, they would have a road to get closer to their timber to fight the fire. So the petition went back into Mr. Dery's office and he signed it with reservation. He said that they should not cut any timber that was not actually needed for the road and not build a right -of -way through there. And we agreed to that, so the road was built.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: No trouble.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He just turned it down and then he changed his mind and approved it on the spot, huh?

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: Yeah, he did.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And so then the survey was made permanent and the road was built.

speaker: Edward Swenson
words: And they complied with that pretty well. They didn't cut trees that were not needed. See, as the road was surveyed, they just cut timber wide enough to form a roadway through. And the road was narrow so that if two trucks met, why, they had to work their way carefully to get by, you know. But it answered the purpose until they got a better road.
